vbox.h1: Rendering the Web Without a Browser
vbox.p: For decades the browser has been the unchallenged king of document rendering. HTML, CSS, and JavaScript form a triad so deeply entrenched that suggesting an alternative feels almost heretical. Yet here we are, parsing XML in a game engine and watching paragraphs, headings, and hyperlinks spring to life as Godot Control nodes.

XML is verbose by design — every tag opens and closes, every attribute is quoted. That verbosity is also its strength. A hand-written XML page is immediately readable, diff-friendly, and trivially parsed by Godot's built-in XMLParser without a single dependency.

Compare that to a full HTML parser: the spec runs to hundreds of pages, error recovery rules alone could fill a dissertation, and you still haven't touched CSS. Our XML dialect sidesteps all of that by defining only the tags we actually need.
Three stages sit between raw text and rendered pixels:
1. XmlPageParser turns the byte stream into a PageNode tree.
2. NodeBuilder walks that tree and emits Godot Controls.
3. AttrApplicator stamps layout properties onto each node.
The result is a live Godot scene indistinguishable from one hand-authored in the editor — except it was built in milliseconds from a plain text file.

Routing was the first logical step beyond a single page. Click "About the Author" in the header and the scene swaps instantly — no browser, no HTTP request, no framework. Just GDScript reading a second XML file from the same folder.
